name: Padmapada
dates_notes: Direct disciple of Śaṅkara per tradition; only the first few pādas of his Pañcapādikā survive.
core_thesis: Padmapāda, Śaṅkara's direct disciple, is the founding figure of what becomes the *Vivaraṇa* sub-school of Advaita. He takes the *adhyāsa-bhāṣya* and the opening *catuḥsūtrī* of Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* and develops them into a positive ontology of *avidyā*. The decisive move is at *Pañcapādikā* V–VI, where he glosses the compound *mithyājñāna* as *mithyā ca tad ajñānaṃ ca* and identifies the *ajñāna*-component with a *jaḍātmikā avidyāśakti* — an ignorance-power whose nature is insentient — that must be admitted in connection with all internal and external things, otherwise the appearance of false objects would be impossible. *Avidyā* (also called *māyā* and *mūla-prakṛti*) is therefore a positive, beginningless (*anādi*) *bhāva-rūpa* entity, not mere absence of knowledge. Its locus (*āśraya*) and object (*viṣaya*) are both *brahman*. It possesses two *śaktis*: *āvaraṇa* (concealment of *brahman*'s *svarūpa*) and *vikṣepa* (projection of the manifest world). The *jīva* is a *pratibimba* (reflection) of *brahman* in the mirror of *avidyā* — the structural alternative to the *avaccheda-vāda* (limitation account) that the *Bhāmatī* line inherits from Maṇḍana. Padmapāda's positions on *avidyā*'s positivity, on *brahman* as its substrate, and on *pratibimba* are foundational for Prakāśātman's *Vivaraṇa* and the entire later *Vivaraṇa* tradition.

engaged_works.title: Pancapadika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored for the surviving portion (first five pādas of Śaṅkara's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya).

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus on Padmapāda's authorship of the surviving portion.

engaged_works.summary: *Ṭīkā* on Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*. The text-historical situation is unusual and critical. Only a portion survives: the commentary on the first five *pādas* of the BSB — that is, the *adhyāsa-bhāṣya* and the *catuḥsūtrī* (BSB 1.1.1–4), plus a small additional stretch. The work's title literally means "commentary on five *pādas*." The traditional account holds that Padmapāda completed a *ṭīkā* on the entire BSB but the manuscript was destroyed, and that Śaṅkara reconstructed what could be reconstructed from oral memory. Whatever the historical truth of that narrative, the surviving portion is doctrinally weight-bearing precisely because it covers the BSB's most metaphysically dense passages — the *adhyāsa-bhāṣya* and the analysis of *tat tu samanvayāt*. Three positions developed in this stretch are taken up wholesale by Prakāśātman in the *Pañcapādikā-Vivaraṇa*: *avidyā* as *bhāva-rūpa* (Pañcapādikā V–VI), the refined *adhyāsa* definition as *paratra pūrvadṛṣṭāvabhāsaḥ smṛtirūpaḥ* — "the appearance elsewhere, in the form of memory, of what was previously seen" (XIX.62–63) — and the *bimba-pratibimba* reading of *tat tvam asi* (XXIX.108). The *Pañcapādikā-Vivaraṇa* becomes the founding text of the sub-school. Securely authored for the surviving portion.

key_passages.locus_long: Pañcapādikā, V.15–VI.16, on mithyājñāna, avidyā-śakti, and the beginningless status of superimposition
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
mithyā ca tad ajñānaṃ ca mithyājñānam |
mithyeti anirvacanīyatocyate |
ajñānam iti ca jaḍātmikāvidyāśaktiḥ
jñānaparyudāsenocyate |
tannimittaḥ tadupādāna ity arthaḥ |
kathaṃ punar naimittikavyavahārasya naisargikatvam |
atrocyate |
avaśyam eṣā avidyāśaktir bāhyādhyātmikeṣu vastuṣu
tatsvarūpasattāmātrānubandhinī abhyupagantavyā
anyathā mithyārthāvabhāsānupapatteḥ |
key_passages.text_critical_note: The dispatched local corpus is an English translation; this Sanskrit sentence is transcribed from a secondary scholarly citation of the Pañcapādikā locus and normalized only for obvious sandhi spacing.

pada_analysis.morphology: 3sg pres ind Ā passive; sandhi of anirvacanīyatā + ucyate

pada_analysis.gloss: the ignorance-power whose nature is insentient

pada_analysis.morphology: 3sg pres ind Ā passive; sandhi of jñānaparyudāsena + ucyate
pada_analysis.gloss: is stated by exclusion of knowledge
having that as its material cause

pada_analysis.morphology: 3sg pres ind Ā passive; sandhi of atra + ucyate

pada_analysis.gloss: connected only with their very own existence-nature
because of the impossibility of the appearance of false objects

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: mithyā ca tad ajñānaṃ ca iti mithyājñānam
samasa_vigrahas.note: Padmapāda himself explicitly decomposes the expression this way.
The internal qualifier jaḍātmikā functions karmadhāraya-like within the larger tatpuruṣa.
Exclusion or negation with respect to knowledge.
Bahuvrīhi-like semantics are compressed into a determinative expression.
Again, the sense is 'having that as material cause'.
A practice or transaction dependent on an occasioning cause.
The power adheres only to sheer existence, not to a fully articulated real essence.

karaka_structure.note: Nominative subject of the passive ucyate in the gloss on mithyā.
Nominative subject of the passive ucyate in the gloss on ajñāna.
Entity to be admitted in abhyupagantavyā.
Domain in which the avidyā-śakti must be admitted.

key_passages.english_close: "Mithyājñāna" is that which is both mithyā and ajñāna. By the term "mithyā", indefinability is stated. And by the term "ajñāna", the power of avidyā — whose own nature is insentient. It is stated, by way of the exclusion of knowledge. "Having that as its cause" means: having that as its material cause. How then can a transactional practice that has an occasioning cause be beginningless by nature? Here it is said as follows. This very power of avidyā must necessarily be admitted. With respect to external and internal things, it must be admitted as connected only with the mere existence that is the own-nature of those things. Otherwise, the manifestation of false objects would be impossible.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: indefinable ignorance; not mere false judgment but ignorance construed as the stuff of superimposition
the power of ignorance, treated here as a positive explanatory factor

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is not a famous isolated slogan; it is the place where Padmapāda explicitly glosses mithyājñāna as an avidyā-śakti and immediately argues that empirical superimposition is therefore beginningless yet causally grounded. That is the decisive doctrinal move from Śaṅkara's adhyāsa-language toward the later Vivaraṇa-style positive ontology of avidyā, and the choice is constrained by the dispatched local corpus being an English translation only.
key_passages.source_edition: Padmapāda, Pañcapādikā V.15–VI.16; locus selected against D. Venkataramiah, trans., The Pancapadika of Padmapada (Baroda: Oriental Institute, 1948), pp. 11–12; Sanskrit wording recovered from a scholarly citation of the Pañcapādikā because the dispatched local corpus is an English translation.

key_passages.locus_long: Pañcapādikā, XIX.62–63, refining the definition of adhyāsa
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
paratra pūrvadṛṣṭāvabhāsaḥ |
paratra smṛtirūpāvabhāsaḥ |
smṛtirūpaḥ paratra pūrvadṛṣṭāvabhāsaḥ |

pada_analysis.gloss: the appearing of what was seen before
appearance having the form of memory
the appearing of what was seen before

samasa_vigrahas.note: The appearance is memory-like, not straightforward recollection.

key_passages.english_close: The appearance elsewhere of what was seen before; the appearance elsewhere of what has the form of memory; [thus, adhyāsa is] the appearance elsewhere, in the form of memory, of what was previously seen.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: superimposition; the erroneous placing of one thing's content in another
memory-formed; presenting itself in the mode of recollection without being literal recollection

key_passages.why_this_passage: This definition is the load-bearing epistemic core of Padmapāda's long adhyāsa analysis. It fairly represents his position because the surrounding discussion tests and refines each limb of the formula rather than merely repeating Śaṅkara's opening phrase, and the local translation volume preserves the Sanskrit definition itself in transliteration.
key_passages.source_edition: Padmapāda, Pañcapādikā XIX.62–63, definition as transliterated in D. Venkataramiah, trans., The Pancapadika of Padmapada (Baroda: Oriental Institute, 1948), pp. 43–44.

key_passages.locus_long: Pañcapādikā, XXIX.108, reading tat tvam asi through the bimba-pratibimba relation
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tat tvam asi |
na tat tvam asīti syāt |
kintu na tvam asīti bhavet
na rajatam astītivat |
key_passages.text_critical_note: The dispatched local corpus gives this argument only in English translation; the Sanskrit clause is restored from a secondary citation of PP 22.21–23, with minimal OCR normalization (bhavet for scan-snippet bhaven).

pada_analysis.morphology: 2sg pres ind P + quotative particle; sandhi of asi + iti
2sg pres ind P + quotative particle; sandhi of asi + iti
3sg pres ind P + quotative particle + indecl comparative; sandhi of asti + iti + vat

karaka_structure.note: Subject of asi in the mahāvākya.
Subject in the counterfactual negated reading na tvam asi.

key_passages.english_close: [The mahāvākya is] "tat tvam asi". It would not [merely amount to] "na tat tvam asi" — "that you are not". Rather, it would amount to "na tvam asi" — "you yourself are not". [And that absurdity would be] just like "there is no silver".

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: reflection; the image-side of the jīva in Padmapāda's model
prototype; the original of which the jīva is the reflection

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the point where the reflection model is made to do semantic work on the mahāvākya itself, not merely illustrative work. It therefore fairly represents Padmapāda's account of the jīva-Brahman relation and of liberating recognition through tat tvam asi; the choice is again constrained by the dispatched local corpus being an English translation.
key_passages.source_edition: Padmapāda, Pañcapādikā XXIX.108; locus selected against D. Venkataramiah, trans., The Pancapadika of Padmapada (Baroda: Oriental Institute, 1948), p. 74; Sanskrit clause verified from a secondary citation of PP 22.21–23 because the dispatched local corpus is an English translation.
